description: Heated 3-speaker debate with frequent interruptions (1000-1800ms overlap). Mix of genders. The kind of pattern that breaks VAD-only segmentation entirely: speakers cut in regularly, no clean silence boundaries between turns. This is the production failure mode the change-point detector has to fix.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Welcome everyone to today's debate on the future of real time speech processing in conference call applications, which is a topic that has been generating a lot of interest lately.

turns.speaker: alice
turns.text: Thanks for having us. I have to say from the start that I think we are vastly underestimating how much improvement is still possible here.

turns.speaker: carlos
turns.text: Hold on Alice, I want to challenge that immediately. The gap between research models and production deployment is huge and we keep ignoring it.

turns.speaker: alice
turns.text: But Carlos that's exactly my point, the production deployment is where most of the wins are.

turns.speaker: carlos
turns.text: Sure but we have to be realistic about what's actually shippable. Latency budgets, compute budgets, language coverage, you name it.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Let's slow down for a second. Alice, can you give us a concrete example of an improvement you think is achievable in the next year?

turns.speaker: alice
turns.text: Yes, real time diarization. We're nowhere near where we should be on multi speaker meetings.

turns.speaker: carlos
turns.text: I disagree completely. The current models are already at human level on clean two speaker audio.

turns.speaker: alice
turns.text: Clean two speaker is not the realistic case Carlos, real meetings are messier than that.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Carlos do you want to respond to that?

turns.speaker: carlos
turns.text: Yes absolutely. I think what Alice is describing is overlap handling specifically, which is a separate hard problem.

turns.speaker: alice
turns.text: Right but that hard problem is the one people actually have to solve.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Let's take a step back. What are the three things you'd both agree are the highest priority improvements?

turns.speaker: carlos
turns.text: Speed of inference and lower latency. That has to be number one for any conference call use case.

turns.speaker: alice
turns.text: Speed yes but only if it doesn't sacrifice accuracy in the cases people actually care about.

turns.speaker: mod
turns.text: Alright, we have to wrap up there. Thanks for a spirited discussion both of you.
